name: Melatonin
summary: A hormone that regulates the sleep–wake cycle; most useful for delayed sleep timing and jet lag.

evidence.claim: Can modestly shorten the time it takes to fall asleep, especially for circadian-rhythm and jet-lag related sleep problems.
Lower doses (0.5–3 mg) are often as effective as higher doses; more is not necessarily better.

name: Magnesium glycinate
summary: An essential mineral; the glycinate form is well tolerated. May support relaxation and sleep quality, especially where intake is low.

evidence.claim: Adequate magnesium status is linked to sleep quality; supplementation may help when dietary intake is insufficient.
The tolerable upper intake level from supplements is 350 mg/day for adults; higher amounts can cause diarrhea.

name: L-theanine
summary: An amino acid found in tea; associated with relaxation without sedation. Sometimes paired with magnesium.

evidence.claim: May promote relaxation and reduce subjective stress, which can indirectly support sleep.

name: Glycine
summary: An amino acid; small studies suggest a dose before bed may improve subjective sleep quality.

evidence.claim: Small trials report improved subjective sleep quality and reduced next-day fatigue with ~3 g before bed.

name: Valerian
summary: A traditional sleep herb. Evidence is mixed; notable for additive sedation with other CNS depressants.

evidence.claim: Some people report improved sleep, but clinical evidence is mixed and study quality varies.

name: Ashwagandha
summary: An adaptogenic herb (root extract) studied for stress and sleep. Not recommended in pregnancy.

evidence.claim: Root-extract studies suggest possible improvements in stress and sleep measures, though trials are small.
